perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance local laws of the State of Florida applicable to agreements made and to be performed within the State, without regard to conflict of laws principles thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of any jurisdiction chosen by Flow Capital Advisors, Inc., without regard to conflict of laws principles thereof.
perturbation.explanation: This modification allows Flow Capital Advisors to choose any jurisdiction's laws, which could conflict with Florida's mandatory application of its own contract laws for agreements performed within the state.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute § 671.301 - Territorial applicability; parties' power to choose applicable law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 671.301(2) allows parties to choose the applicable law only when the transaction bears a reasonable relation to the chosen state. The modified text allows Flow to choose any jurisdiction regardless of its relation to the contract, potentially overriding Florida law where it should apply.

F.S. 671.301 671.301 Effective date; provision for transition; preservation of old transition provision. — (1) This act shall take effect at 12:01 a.m. on January 1, 1980. (2) Transactions validly entered into after January 1, 1967, and before the effective date of this act, and which were subject to the provisions of chapter 65-254, Laws of Florida, as amended, and which would be subject to this act as amended if they had been entered into after the effective date of this act and the rights, duties, and interests flowing from such transactions remain valid after the latter date and may be terminated, completed, consummated, or enforced as required or permitted by this act. Security interests arising out of such transactions which are perfected when this act becomes effective shall remain perfected until they lapse as provided in this act and may be continued as permitted by this act, except as stated in s. 680.108 , Florida Statutes 1979. (3) The provisions of s. 680.101 , Florida Statutes 1977, shall continue to apply to this act and for this purpose the two statutes shall be considered one continuous statute. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254; s. 1, ch. 69-218; s. 36, ch. 79-398; s. 6, ch. 90-278. Note. — Former s. 680.101.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates Florida law, while the changed text allows Flow Capital Advisors to select any jurisdiction's laws. According to the Law Explanation, Florida Statute § 671.301(2) permits parties to choose the applicable law only if the transaction has a reasonable connection to the chosen state. The modification potentially overrides Florida law, even when it should apply, which creates a contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Florida Statute 671.301, supports the legal basis for the original clause's intention to apply Florida law, and the change allows circumvention of this.

perturbation.original_text: This is an agreement between separate legal entities and neither is the agent or employee of the other for any purpose whatsoever. The parties do not intend to create a partnership or joint venture between themselves. Neither party shall have the right to bind the other to any agreement with a third party or to inure any obligation or liabilities on behalf of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: This is an agreement between separate legal entities, but Flow Capital Advisors, Inc. shall act as Jag Media Holdings, Inc.'s agent in all transactions with Introduced Parties. The parties do not intend to create a partnership or joint venture between themselves. Flow Capital Advisors, Inc. shall have the right to bind Jag Media Holdings, Inc. to any agreement with a third party regarding Introduced Parties.
perturbation.explanation: This modification states that Flow Capital Advisors acts as Jag Media Holdings' agent, which contradicts the original intention that neither party is an agent of the other, potentially creating unintended liability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute § 671.201(3) - Agency definition and establishment
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 671.201(3) defines agency and its establishment. The original contract explicitly denies agency, whereas the modified version establishes it, contradicting the expressed intent and potentially violating principles of agency law requiring clear consent and understanding of responsibilities.

F.S. 671.201 671.201 General definitions. — Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other chapters of this code which apply to particular chapters or parts thereof, have the meanings stated. Subject to definitions contained in other chapters of this code which apply to particular chapters or parts thereof, the term: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceedings in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of dealing, usage of trade, or course of performance as provided in ss. 671.205 and 672.208 . (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking and includes a savings bank, a savings and loan association, a credit union, and a trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person in control of a negotiable electronic document of title or a person in possession of a negotiable instrument, a negotiable tangible document of title, or a certificated security that is payable to bearer or indorsed in blank. (6) “Bill of lading” means a document of title evidencing the receipt of goods for shipment issued by a person engaged in the business of directly or indirectly transporting or forwarding goods. The term does not include a warehouse receipt. (7) “Branch” includes a separately incorporated foreign branch of a bank. (8) “Burden of establishing” a fact means the burden of persuading the triers of fact that the existence of the fact is more probable than its nonexistence. (9) “Buyer in ordinary course of business” means a person who, in ordinary course, buys goods in good faith, without knowledge that the sale violates the rights of another person in the goods, from a person, other tha
perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly states that neither party is an agent of the other, preventing one party from binding the other to agreements or liabilities. The changed text directly contradicts this by stating that Flow Capital Advisors will act as Jag Media Holdings' agent and have the right to bind them to agreements, creating a principal-agent relationship where none was intended. This contradicts the initial agreement that neither party could act on behalf of the other. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation state that this change goes against principles of agency law, and Florida Statute § 671.201 defines general terms. Although, Statute § 671.201 does not explicitly define "agency", it is reasonable to assume that this definition is important in how agency is to be established in Florida contracts, thus any contradiction would be impactful.

perturbation.original_text: Pursuant to discussions between Flow and Jag, Flow has disclosed to Jag that certain parties, some of whom who have been identified to Jag and others who have yet to be identified by Flow to Jag (the “Introduced Parties”), may be interested in entering into certain transactions with Jag.
perturbation.changed_text: Pursuant to discussions between Flow and Jag, Flow has disclosed to Jag that certain parties (the “Introduced Parties”), may be interested in entering into certain transactions with Jag. The term 'Introduced Parties' will refer to anyone Flow claims to have introduced, regardless of prior relationships or knowledge.
perturbation.explanation: The modification broadens the definition of 'Introduced Parties' to include individuals Jag already knows or has relationships with, potentially violating fair business practices by giving Flow undue credit for introductions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida's Unfair and Deceptive Trade Practices Act (FDUTPA) § 501.204
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 501.204 prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. Broadly defining 'Introduced Parties' to include pre-existing relationships could be seen as an unfair business practice, potentially violating FDUTPA.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIII > Chapter 501 > Section 204 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIII REGULATION OF TRADE, COMMERCE, INVESTMENTS, AND SOLICITATIONS Chapter 501 CONSUMER PROTECTION Entire Chapter SECTION 204 Unlawful acts and practices. F.S. 501.204 501.204 Unlawful acts and practices. — (1) Unfair methods of competition, unconscionable acts or practices, and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (2) It is the intent of the Legislature that, in construing subsection (1), due consideration and great weight shall be given to the interpretations of the Federal Trade Commission and the federal courts relating to s. 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act, 15 U.S.C. s. 45(a)(1) as of July 1, 2017. History. — s. 1, ch. 73-124; s. 1, ch. 83-117; s. 4, ch. 85-63; s. 2, ch. 90-190; s. 3, ch. 93-38; s. 2, ch. 2001-39; s. 23, ch. 2001-214; s. 2, ch. 2006-196; s. 4, ch. 2013-207; s. 5, ch. 2015-92; s. 4, ch. 2017-155.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text expands the definition of 'Introduced Parties' to include those with pre-existing relationships, which the Law Explanation suggests could be an unfair business practice. Florida Statute § 501.204(1), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, declares unlawful "Unfair methods of competition, unconscionable acts or practices, and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce." The altered definition, by potentially giving Flow credit for pre-existing relationships, could be seen as an unfair method of competition, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Jag agrees that once Flow has disclosed the Identity of any Introduced Party to Jag, Jag, its officers, directors, shareholders, employees and agents shall not have any contacts with the Introduced Party other than through Flow, unless Flow grants permission in writing for such contacts.
perturbation.changed_text: Jag agrees that once Flow *claims* to have disclosed the Identity of any Introduced Party to Jag, Jag, its officers, directors, shareholders, employees and agents shall not have any contacts with the Introduced Party other than through Flow, unless Flow grants permission in writing for such contacts. Flow's claim shall be deemed conclusive and not subject to dispute.
perturbation.explanation: This change gives Flow the unilateral power to restrict Jag's contact with any party simply by claiming they were 'introduced,' regardless of actual introduction or prior relationships. It contradicts principles of good faith and fair dealing implied in contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Uniform Commercial Code § 1-304 - Obligation of good faith
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 671.304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of every contract. Granting Flow conclusive and indisputable power over defining 'introduction' violates this duty of good faith, as it permits arbitrary and potentially abusive control over Jag's business relationships.

F.S. 671.304 671.304 Laws not repealed; precedence where code provisions in conflict with other laws; certain statutory remedies retained. — (1) The article on documents of title (Art. 7) does not repeal or modify any laws prescribing the form or contents of documents of title or the services or facilities to be afforded by bailees, or otherwise regulating bailees’ businesses in respects not specifically dealt with herein; but the fact that such laws are violated does not affect the status of a document of title which otherwise complies with the definition of a “document of title” (s. 671.201 ). (2) The following laws and parts of laws are specifically not repealed and shall take precedence over any provisions of this code which may be inconsistent or in conflict therewith: (a) Chapter 517 — Sale of securities. (b) Chapter 687 — Interest and usury; lending practices. (c) Chapter 516 — Florida Consumer Finance Act. (d) Chapter 520 — Retail installment sales (Part I, Motor Vehicle Sales Finance Act; Part II, Retail Installment Sales Act; Part III, Installment Sales Finance Act). (e) Chapter 665 — Florida Savings Association Act. (f) Chapter 657 — Credit unions. (g) Chapters 319 and 328 — Title certificates (motor vehicle; vessel). (h) Chapter 538 — Secondhand Dealers and Secondary Metals Recyclers. (i) Chapters 658, 660, and 663. (3) The following laws or parts of laws, although not repealed, shall yield to and be superseded by any provisions of the code which may be inconsistent or in conflict therewith: (a) Chapter 697 — Instruments deemed mortgages and the nature of a mortgage. (b) Chapter 701 — Assignment and cancellation of mortgages. (c) Chapter 702 — Foreclosure of mortgages. (d) Chapter 727 — General assignments. (4) Notwithstanding any provisions to the contrary in any of the following Florida Statutes, the remedies provided by such statutes shall not restrict the remedies otherwise available to a secured party under this code, but all such remedies shall be cumulatively
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by allowing Flow to unilaterally determine if an 'introduction' occurred, binding Jag based solely on Flow's claim, regardless of the actual circumstances. This undermines the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, as Flow could arbitrarily restrict Jag's business relationships. The LLM-generated law explanation references Florida Statute § 671.304, which imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of every contract. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows Florida Statute 671.304, existing in the Florida Statutes. By granting Flow conclusive and indisputable power, the changed contract text violates this obligation, as it permits arbitrary and potentially abusive control over Jag's business relationships, thereby establishing a legal contradiction.
